agent_output: {"executive_summary":"For a small courier team (3 engineers) needing reliable, extensible delivery notifications with a 5-minute latency budget, Option B (event-driven with a queue) is the clear winner. It naturally decouples the delivery domain from notification concerns, makes channel addition trivial, and solves the reliability requirement structurally (queue-based retry with dead-letter) rather than bolting it on. The latency budget of 5 minutes removes the only argument for synchronous calls. However, the team size constraint means we must choose a managed queue (SQS, Cloud Tasks, or BullMQ on Redis) rather than self-hosted Kafka or RabbitMQ.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Delivery Service","responsibility":"Owns delivery lifecycle (picked up, in transit, delivered, failed). Publishes domain events when status changes. Zero knowledge of notification channels.","boundaries":"Knows nothing about email, SMS, or any notification concern. Its only contract is publishing a DeliveryStatusChanged event to the queue."},{"name":"Message Queue","responsibility":"Durable buffer between delivery events and notification dispatch. Provides at-least-once delivery, retry with backoff, and dead-letter for poison messages.","boundaries":"Managed service (SQS, Cloud Tasks, or BullMQ on Redis). Not self-hosted. The team does not operate broker infrastructure."},{"name":"Notification Worker","responsibility":"Consumes DeliveryStatusChanged events, resolves customer preferences (channel, opt-in), and dispatches to the appropriate channel adapter. Handles per-channel retry logic.","boundaries":"Stateless worker process. Owns the notification sending logic but delegates actual delivery to channel adapters. Owns the notification log/audit table."},{"name":"Channel Adapters","responsibility":"Thin wrappers around external provider APIs (SendGrid, Twilio, etc.). Translate a canonical NotificationPayload into provider-specific API calls.","boundaries":"Each adapter implements a common ChannelAdapter interface. No business logic — only protocol translation and error normalization."},{"name":"Customer Preferences Store","responsibility":"Stores customer notification preferences: opted-in channels, contact details, quiet hours.","boundaries":"Read-only from the notification worker's perspective. Written by a separate customer-facing settings flow."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Delivery Service","to":"Message Queue","contract":"Publishes DeliveryStatusChanged { deliveryId, customerId, status, timestamp, metadata }","coupling_type":"loose — fire-and-forget publish via queue SDK"},{"from":"Message Queue","to":"Notification Worker","contract":"At-least-once delivery of DeliveryStatusChanged events with visibility timeout and DLQ","coupling_type":"loose — consumer pulls from queue, no direct coupling to producer"},{"from":"Notification Worker","to":"Channel Adapters","contract":"ChannelAdapter.send(notification: NotificationPayload): Result<Success, RetryableError | PermanentError>","coupling_type":"loose — strategy pattern behind a common interface, resolved at runtime from customer preferences"},{"from":"Channel Adapters","to":"External Providers","contract":"Provider-specific REST/SDK calls (SendGrid API, Twilio API, etc.)","coupling_type":"tight to provider API — isolated inside adapter boundary so the rest of the system is unaffected by provider changes"},{"from":"Notification Worker","to":"Customer Preferences Store","contract":"getPreferences(customerId): { channels: Channel[], contactDetails, quietHours }","coupling_type":"data dependency — synchronous read"}],"data_flow":"Delivery Service → (publishes event) → Message Queue → (worker pulls) → Notification Worker → (reads preferences) → Customer Preferences Store → (dispatches via) → Channel Adapter → External Provider. Failed sends: re-queued with exponential backoff. Poison messages: routed to Dead Letter Queue for manual inspection.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Domain Events","rationale":"The delivery status change is a genuine domain event — something meaningful happened in the business. Publishing it decouples the delivery bounded context from the notification context completely."},{"pattern":"Queue-based Load Leveling","rationale":"The 5-minute latency budget means we can buffer events and process them at a sustainable rate. This protects the notification workers from traffic spikes (e.g., a batch of 10,000 deliveries completing simultaneously)."},{"pattern":"Strategy Pattern (Channel Adapters)","rationale":"Each notification channel implements the same interface. Adding WhatsApp or push notifications means writing a new adapter and registering it — zero changes to the worker or delivery service."},{"pattern":"Dead Letter Queue","rationale":"Failed notifications that exhaust retries land in a DLQ rather than being silently dropped. This directly addresses the high-reliability requirement and gives the team a clear recovery mechanism."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Consumer","rationale":"At-least-once delivery means duplicates are possible. Each notification carries a deduplication key (deliveryId + status + channel) to prevent customers from receiving the same SMS twice."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["Delivery (root: deliveryId, tracks status transitions, emits DeliveryStatusChanged events)"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRecord (root: notificationId, tracks dispatch status per channel per event, enables idempotency and audit)","CustomerPreferences (root: customerId, stores channel opt-ins and contact details)"]},{"name":"Channel Integration","classification":"generic","aggregates":["No domain aggregates — stateless adapters wrapping third-party APIs"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Delivery","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via domain events. Delivery context publishes events in its own language; Notification context consumes them. The event schema is the published language contract."},{"upstream":"Notification","downstream":"Channel Integration","relationship":"Open Host Service. Notification context defines the ChannelAdapter interface; each integration conforms to it."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Option B: Event-driven with queue over Option A: Synchronous REST","gains":"Decouples delivery from notification concerns. Structural reliability via queue persistence and retry. Adding channels requires zero changes to delivery service. Traffic spikes are absorbed by the queue. Partial failures in one channel don't block others.","sacrifices":"Adds a queue dependency (infrastructure component). Slightly more complex local development setup. Eventual consistency — notification is not confirmed at the time the delivery status changes. Harder to debug end-to-end flow than a simple synchronous call.","alternatives_considered":["Option A: Synchronous REST — simpler initially but couples delivery service to every notification channel, makes retry the delivery service's problem, and requires the delivery service to change every time a channel is added. Rejected because it violates separation of concerns and makes reliability hard.","Hybrid: Synchronous for email, async for others — adds worst-of-both-worlds complexity with no clear benefit. Rejected."]},{"decision":"Managed queue service over self-hosted broker","gains":"Zero operational burden for a 3-person team. Built-in retry, DLQ, monitoring. No patching, no capacity planning for the broker itself.","sacrifices":"Vendor coupling to a specific cloud provider. Slightly higher per-message cost than self-hosted. Less control over tuning.","alternatives_considered":["Self-hosted RabbitMQ — powerful but requires operational expertise the team doesn't have. Rejected for team size constraint.","Self-hosted Kafka — massive overkill for notification volume. Rejected.","BullMQ on Redis — viable middle ground if the team already runs Redis. Lower vendor lock-in but more operational responsibility than SQS/Cloud Tasks."]},{"decision":"Per-channel retry with exponential backoff rather than immediate retry","gains":"Respects provider rate limits. Avoids thundering herd on transient failures. Gives providers time to recover.","sacrifices":"Increases worst-case notification latency. A message that fails 3 times with backoff could take several minutes — but this is within the 5-minute budget.","alternatives_considered":["Immediate retry with circuit breaker — faster recovery but risks hitting rate limits and being blocked by providers. Rejected as unnecessarily aggressive given the latency budget."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Channel Adapters","evolution_path":"New channels (push, WhatsApp, Slack) are added by implementing the ChannelAdapter interface and registering in config. No code changes elsewhere."},{"area":"Event Schema","evolution_path":"The DeliveryStatusChanged event can be versioned. New fields are added as optional. A schema registry or version field in the event envelope supports forward compatibility."},{"area":"Notification Routing Logic","evolution_path":"Currently simple preference lookup. Can evolve to rules engine (e.g., send SMS for 'delivered', email for 'delayed', push for 'out for delivery') without changing the adapter layer."},{"area":"Queue Technology","evolution_path":"The queue is accessed through a thin abstraction. Migrating from SQS to Cloud Tasks or BullMQ requires changing one module, not the entire system."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"DeliveryStatusChanged Event Contract","reason":"This is the published language between Delivery and Notification contexts. Changing its shape is a coordinated change across two systems. This rigidity is intentional — stable contracts enable independent evolution of both sides."},{"area":"At-Least-Once Delivery Semantics","reason":"The system assumes at-least-once and builds idempotency on top. Switching to exactly-once or at-most-once would require rethinking the deduplication strategy. This is the right choice for notifications — a duplicate SMS is annoying but a dropped one is a support ticket."},{"area":"ChannelAdapter Interface","reason":"The send() contract is deliberately simple and stable. All complexity is pushed inside each adapter. Changing this interface ripples to every adapter implementation."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Poison message loop: a malformed event that crashes the worker on every attempt, consuming retry budget forever","severity":"high","mitigation":"Dead Letter Queue with max retry count (e.g., 3 attempts). DLQ monitoring alert. Manual review + replay tooling for DLQ messages."},{"risk":"Silent notification loss if the queue itself is misconfigured (e.g., message TTL too short, DLQ not wired)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Infrastructure-as-code for queue config. Integration test that publishes an event and verifies it reaches the worker. DLQ depth alarm."},{"risk":"Provider outage causes all notifications on that channel to back up and exhaust queue capacity","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Circuit breaker per channel adapter. When a provider is down, fail fast and rely on queue retry with backoff. Alert the team so they can switch providers if extended."},{"risk":"Customer receives duplicate notifications due to at-least-once delivery","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Idempotency key (deliveryId + status + channel) stored in NotificationRecord. Worker checks before sending. Even if dedup fails, a duplicate 'Your package was delivered' SMS is a minor annoyance, not a business-critical failure."},{"risk":"Overengineering for a 3-person team — the event-driven architecture becomes a maintenance burden","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a managed queue with zero-ops overhead. Keep the worker as a simple polling loop, not a complex framework. Start with one worker process and scale only when needed. The architecture is simple in practice — publish, consume, dispatch — despite sounding complex in description."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Start with a single Notification Worker process consuming from a managed queue (AWS SQS or BullMQ on Redis if self-hosting). Implement email and SMS channel adapters first. Use a simple database table (notification_log) for idempotency and audit. Deploy the worker as a long-running process alongside the delivery service. No need for Kubernetes, containers, or microservices — a single deployable with the worker running in a separate process is sufficient for this team size.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/delivery/events/DeliveryStatusChanged.ts — event schema definition","src/notification/worker.ts — queue consumer, preference lookup, dispatch orchestration","src/notification/adapters/ChannelAdapter.ts — interface definition","src/notification/adapters/EmailAdapter.ts — SendGrid/SES integration","src/notification/adapters/SmsAdapter.ts — Twilio integration","src/notification/models/NotificationRecord.ts — idempotency and audit log entity","src/notification/models/CustomerPreferences.ts — channel preferences entity","src/notification/queue.ts — thin queue abstraction (publish/consume/ack/nack)","infra/queue-config.tf or similar IaC — queue, DLQ, retry policy, alarms","tests/notification/worker.test.ts — unit tests with fake queue and fake adapters","tests/notification/integration.test.ts — end-to-end: publish event → verify adapter called"],"patterns_to_follow":"Strategy pattern for channel adapters. Idempotent consumer for deduplication. Thin queue abstraction to avoid vendor lock-in. Domain events as the integration contract. Keep the worker simple — a loop that pulls, processes, and acks. No framework magic.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put notification logic inside the Delivery Service — this couples domains and makes the delivery service's failure modes worse","Do NOT build a custom retry mechanism — use the queue's native retry and DLQ support","Do NOT start with multiple worker instances — one is enough until proven otherwise, and it avoids concurrency/ordering issues","Do NOT skip the idempotency check — at-least-once delivery WILL produce duplicates, and customers WILL notice duplicate SMS charges","Do NOT use an in-memory queue (e.g., a simple array) for 'simplicity' — it defeats the entire reliability guarantee. Use a real persistent queue from day one","Do NOT abstract over queue AND provider simultaneously in a 'notification framework' — keep it simple, you're 3 engineers not 30"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":90,"high_confidence_areas":["Event-driven (Option B) is the correct choice given the requirements — the latency budget, reliability requirement, and extensibility need all point the same direction","Managed queue over self-hosted is correct for a 3-person team","Strategy pattern for channel adapters is the right abstraction level","Dead letter queue is essential for the reliability requirement"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific queue technology choice depends on the team's existing cloud provider and infrastructure, which is not specified","Whether BullMQ on Redis vs SQS vs Cloud Tasks is best depends on existing stack"],"assumptions":["The team has access to a cloud provider with a managed queue service or already runs Redis","Notification volume is moderate (thousands to tens of thousands per day, not millions)","The delivery service already exists and can be modified to publish events","Customer preferences are stored somewhere accessible to the notification worker"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"For a small courier team (3 engineers) needing reliable, extensible delivery notifications with a 5-minute latency budget, Option B (event-driven with a queue) is the clear winner. It naturally decouples the delivery domain from notification concerns, makes channel addition trivial, and solves the reliability requirement structurally (queue-based retry with dead-letter) rather than bolting it on. The latency budget of 5 minutes removes the only argument for synchronous call
